content:
8 min read
Just now

--

The realization that transformed me from a struggling developer into someone who actually enjoyed writing code.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Flipsnack on UnsplashWhen I first started coding, I thought great programmers had incredible memories.
They remembered every syntax. Every function. Every algorithm. Every library. Every framework.
I couldn’t understand how.
I’d watch YouTube tutorials where someone would rattle off method names without hesitation, and I’d think: How?
I spent hours trying to memorize Python’s standard library. I made flashcards for JavaScript array methods. I wrote out Java’s collection framework by hand until my wrist ached.
Then one day I watched a senior developer solve a bug in less than five minutes. A bug that had taken me two days to diagnose.
He didn’t look up documentation. He didn’t Google anything. He just… looked at the code, nodded to himself, changed three characters, and the whole thing worked.
“Wait,” I said. “How did you know that?”
He shrugged. “I’ve seen this before.”
That was it. That was the entire answer.
He wasn’t remembering code. He was recognizing a pattern.

I wish I could tell you I had my epiphany right there and immediately became a better programmer.
I didn’t.
I nodded along, pretended to understand, and went back to memorizing syntax.
Because that’s what I thought the path looked like. Memorize enough. Practice enough. Eventually, I’d just know things the way he did.
Meanwhile, I kept falling into the same traps.
I’d watch a tutorial on building a REST API. I’d follow along. I’d get it working. Then I’d try to build one myself and completely freeze.
I’d forgotten the exact order of operations. The specific annotations. The precise syntax. So I’d go back to the tutorial, copy the code, move on.
Then I’d wonder why I wasn’t actually learning anything.
You probably know exactly what I’m talking about.

Tutorial hell is a real place, and it’s crowded.
You go from course to course, video to video, project to project. You build the same todo app in six different frameworks. You read documentation for hours. You copy Stack Overflow answers and feel guilty about it.
And all the while, that voice in your head whispers: You’re not a real developer. You can’t even remember how to write a for loop in C++ without looking it up.
I spent two years in that loop. Two years of feeling like I was learning something, only to realize I couldn’t build anything without hand-holding.
The worst part?
I had this persistent fear that someone would discover my secret. That a senior dev would look at my code and know I didn’t actually understand what I was doing. That I was just a very good faker.
If you’ve felt that, I want you to read the next part very carefully.

Here’s the thing about programming that nobody tells you in a bootcamp or CS degree:
Programming is pattern recognition. Syntax is simply one way to express a pattern.
That’s it.
I know it doesn’t sound revolutionary. But when I truly understood this, everything changed.
A for loop in Python looks like for item in list:
In Java, it's for (int i = 0; i < list.length; i++)
In JavaScript, it's for (let item of list)
These look different. They use different words and punctuation.
But they all express the exact same idea:
Go through each item in this collection and do something with it.
The language changes. The pattern doesn’t.
Once you can separate the pattern from its expression, you stop worrying about syntax. You start seeing the underlying structure. And suddenly, picking up a new language isn’t scary anymore.

Think about learning to drive.
When you first start, you’re overwhelmed. Clutch. Gear stick. Steering wheel. Indicators. Mirrors. Speed. Traffic. Pedestrians. It feels impossible.
But after a few years? You don’t think about any of it. You just get in the car and go.
When you see a junction, you don’t consciously think: Okay, I need to slow down, check my mirrors, signal, shift to second gear, turn the wheel…
You just turn.
Why? Because driving is pattern recognition. You’ve seen so many junctions that your brain automatically handles the details.
Programming is exactly the same.
The first time you see a binary search, it’s confusing. The second time, less so. By the tenth time, you don’t even think about it. You just know: This problem needs a binary search.

Let me give you a concrete example.
Here’s a problem: Given an array of numbers, find the maximum value.
In Python:
max_val = arr[0]
for num in arr:
if num > max_val:
max_val = numIn Java:
int max = arr[0];
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
if (arr[i] > max) {
max = arr[i];
}
}In JavaScript:
let max = arr[0];
for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
if (arr[i] > max) {
max = arr[i];
}
}Three languages. Three different syntaxes. One pattern: Iterate and compare.
When I learned to see the pattern instead of memorizing the code, I stopped caring about whether I was writing Python or Java. The logic was the same. The words were just different.

Now here’s how senior developers think.
They don’t see code. They see abstractions.
When they see for (or any iteration construct), they think: Iteration.
When they see a function calling itself, they think: Divide and conquer.
When they see nested conditionals, they think: Decision tree.
When they see SQL joins, they think: Relationships.
When they see a function with a specific signature, they think: Interface.
This is what experience builds. It’s not that you memorized more syntax. It’s that you’ve seen enough problems to recognize their shape immediately.
A junior developer sees code. A senior developer sees the problem the code is solving.

There’s actually psychology behind this. It’s called chunking.
When you first learn something, every detail is separate. Each piece takes mental effort.
Think about chess. A beginner sees individual pieces. A grandmaster sees patterns of attacks and defenses. The grandmaster isn’t smarter — they’ve just seen more positions. They’ve chunked the board into familiar configurations.
Same with programming. When you’ve solved enough array problems, you stop thinking about each step. You just see: This is a two-pointer problem. This is a sliding window. This is a hash map situation.
These patterns become mental shortcuts. Your brain recognizes them automatically.
That’s how the senior developer solved my bug in five minutes. He’d seen it before. The pattern was already chunked in his memory. He didn’t have to think — he just knew.

Let’s bust some myths while we’re here.
Myth 1: You need to memorize syntax.
No. You need to understand patterns. Syntax is the language you use to express them. Expressing the same idea in different ways is much easier than memorizing every possible expression.
Myth 2: You need to know every programming language.
No. You need to understand fundamental patterns. Once you do, picking up a new language takes a couple of weeks. The patterns are the same. The syntax is just different.
Myth 3: Senior developers never Google.
This one makes me laugh. I’ve seen senior developers with twenty years of experience Google how to reverse an array. Nobody remembers everything. The difference is that they know exactly what they’re looking for.
Myth 4: My memory is bad, so I can’t be a good programmer.
Your memory isn’t the problem. Your exposure is. The more patterns you see, the less you have to remember. Your brain does the work automatically.

Here’s the shift that changed everything for me.
I stopped trying to memorize code. I started trying to understand problems.
When I opened a new tutorial, I didn’t copy the code. I paused and asked: What problem is this solving?
Then I looked at the solution and asked: What pattern is this using?
Then, and only then, did I look at the actual code.
I spent more time thinking and less time typing. And I learned faster than I ever had.
I wish someone had told me this earlier. I spent months chasing syntax instead of understanding ideas. I memorized Python list comprehensions but didn’t actually understand what they were doing. It was a waste of time.

Let me give you a practical way to practice this.
When you solve a problem, don’t just write the code and move on. Write down the pattern in plain English.
Example:
Problem: Find if a string contains a palindrome.
Pattern: Two pointers moving inward.
Expression: In Python, while left < right:. In Java, the same. In C++, the same.
That’s it. That’s the whole thing.
Do this enough times, and you’ll start seeing the patterns before you even think about the language. The code becomes secondary.

The biggest difference between beginners and experts isn’t intelligence.
It isn’t memory.
It isn’t natural talent.
It’s the number of patterns they’ve seen.
And the beautiful thing about patterns?
Every problem you solve adds another one.
Every bug you fix teaches you something new.
Every code review shows you a different way to think.
Every language you learn broadens your mental library.
The more you see, the easier it gets. It’s a virtuous cycle that rewards curiosity.

So if you’re struggling right now — if you’re in tutorial hell, if you feel like you’ll never “get it,” if you’re comparing yourself to developers who seem to know everything — I want you to understand something.
You’re not learning wrong. You’re just learning at a different level.
Right now, you’re memorizing syntax. And that’s okay — everyone starts there.
But the goal is to start seeing through the syntax. To recognize the shapes beneath. To build the mental library that makes code feel intuitive.
That takes time. That takes exposure. That takes practice.
But it doesn’t take a perfect memory.
It just takes seeing enough patterns that they start to stick.

Here’s what I want you to do next.
Pick a problem. Any problem.
Write the solution in your language of choice.
Then write the solution in two other languages.
Don’t look up the syntax. Try to remember it. If you can’t, look it up.
But then ask yourself: Did the logic change? Or just the words?
If the logic stayed the same, congratulations. You’ve just proven to yourself that programming isn’t about memorizing syntax.
It’s about recognizing patterns.
And you already knew that pattern. You just had to see it.

One more thing.
The next time you feel like an imposter — like you don’t belong, like everyone else knows something you don’t — remember that senior developer from earlier.
He didn’t know everything. He just knew that one bug. Because he’d seen it before.
He wasn’t smarter than me. He was just more experienced.
And experience is just the accumulation of patterns.
Patterns you’re building right now.
Every line of code. Every bug. Every Google search.
They’re all adding to your library.
Keep going. You’re closer than you think.
